title: Dyeing with apple bark
subtitle: A warm tan to brown dye from apple tree bark on alum-mordanted wool, with an iron modifier for olive and grey tones
excerpt: Apple bark (Malus domestica and related species) is a useful winter dye material: pruning provides bark without harming the tree, and the bark stores well when dried. It produces warm tan to rich brown on alum-mordanted wool, shifting to olive-grey with an iron modifier. This entry covers bark preparation, mordanting, dyeing, and the iron modification step.
sourceNotes: Apple bark natural dyeing synthesised from general natural dye references. Bark from Malus species contains tannins and other compounds that produce warm brown tones on protein fibres.

glossaryTerms.definition: A mineral salt used to bond dye permanently to fibre. Potassium alum gives warm tan tones on apple bark; iron shifts the colour toward olive and grey.
The dry weight of the fibre to be dyed, used as the reference for all recipe quantities. Abbreviated WOF.

A solution of ferrous sulphate (iron) added to the dye bath or used as an after-bath to shift the colour toward a greener, darker, or more muted tone. Called saddening in traditional natural dye terminology.
A naturally occurring compound found in bark, leaves, and other plant materials that bonds directly to wool without a mordant and contributes brown and tan tones.

attrs.title: Iron mordant safety
attrs.body: Ferrous sulphate irritates skin and eyes. Wear gloves and avoid breathing dust. Keep iron equipment separate from food use.

content.text: Apple tree bark is a practical zero-waste dye material: the prunings from an annual apple tree tidy produce enough bark for several dye sessions when dried and stored. The
tannin
content means apple bark gives colour even without a
mordant
, though an alum mordant deepens and fixes the colour considerably. An
iron modifier
applied after dyeing shifts the warm tan toward a complex olive-grey, useful for natural-palette projects. Use 100-150%
weight of fibre
of dried bark for a good result.

items.name: Dried apple bark, chipped or broken into small pieces
Stainless steel dye pot, 6 litre capacity

content.text: Method
Step 1: Prepare the bark

Harvest bark from pruned apple branches: strip the outer bark from branches at least 1 cm in diameter. Dry thoroughly before storing (damp bark moulds quickly). When ready to dye, break or chip the dried bark into pieces no larger than 2-3 cm and soak overnight in cold water. This pre-soak softens the bark and begins releasing the dye compounds before heating.
Step 2: Mordant the fibre

Dissolve the alum in hot water and add to a separate pot with enough water to submerge the skein. Add the pre-wetted wool. Raise to 85°C over 30 minutes and hold for 45 minutes. Allow to cool in the mordant bath before lifting the skein.
Step 3: Prepare the dye bath

Simmer the soaked bark in its soaking water for 60 minutes at 85°C to extract the maximum colour. The bath will be a deep amber-brown. Strain out the bark pieces through a sieve and return the liquid to the pot. Apple bark releases colour slowly: a longer extraction time gives a stronger bath.
Step 4: Dye the fibre

Add the mordanted skein to the strained bark bath. Heat slowly to 80-85°C over 20 minutes. Hold for 45-60 minutes, stirring gently. Allow the skein to cool in the bath before rinsing. The colour will be warm tan to mid-brown depending on the bark and the water hardness.
Step 5: Apply the iron modifier (optional)

Dissolve 2 g ferrous sulphate in a small amount of warm water. Add to a separate small pot of hot water with the rinsed skein. Simmer gently for 10 minutes. The warm tan will shift toward olive-grey as the iron complexes with the tannin compounds in the fibre. Rinse thoroughly in two changes of warm water immediately after the iron step: prolonged contact with iron weakens wool fibres over time.

items.problem: The colour is pale despite using enough bark
items.cause: The bark was not soaked long enough before extraction, and the dye compounds were not fully released
items.fix: Soak the bark for a full 24 hours before simmering. Repeat the extraction with the same bark for a second 60-minute simmer and combine the two baths.

items.problem: The iron modifier has turned the colour black rather than olive
items.cause: Too much ferrous sulphate was used
items.fix: Use no more than 2% WOF of iron (2 g per 100 g fibre). Iron overdoses darken and can eventually damage wool: err on the side of too little.

items.problem: The finished yarn feels harsh after the iron step
items.cause: The iron contact time was too long, beginning to degrade the wool fibres
items.fix: Keep the iron step to 10 minutes maximum and rinse promptly and thoroughly. Add a splash of white vinegar to the final rinse water to neutralise any remaining iron.
